coredata.dc:title: Sensitive and reliable hybrid nanosensor (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detecting NO2 −
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

Zhang, Panqing
Zhao, Xiuqing

Li, Xue
Zhang, Zeyu
Yang, Yunxu
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detecting assay for NO2 − was realized by a hybrid nanosensor (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) utilizing firstly through the redox reaction of nitrite (NO2 −) with Co2+, of which the hybrid nanosensor Co2+-CDs@R-CDs was fabricated by Co2+-doped carbon dots (Co2+-CDs) and a reference of red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The ratiometric fluorescent linear detection range of NO2 − was 2.5–45 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.068 μM with the response time of 120 s. While, the colorimetric linear detection range of NO2 − was 2.5–60 μM and the LOD was 0.075 μM. In addition, a portable smartphone system which could measure the R (red), G (green), and B (blue) values of the fluorescence and the visible color of the coated Co2+-CDs@R-CDs paper strip-based sensor had also been developed and successfully applied to detect NO2 − in sausage, pickles and tap water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrite
Carbon nanodots
Ratiometric fluorescence
Colorimetric
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs and the optimization condition for detecting NO2 -
UV–vis and fluorescent measurements of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 -
Selectivity and specificity of detecting NO2 -
Portable detection of NO2 − via smartphone APP in double modes
Analysis of NO2 − in actual samples
Structural characterization and optical properties of R-CDs
Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of NO2 -
Selectivity and anti-interference of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 − in solution

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and preparation procedures of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs.

(A) FTIR spectrum (B) The TEM image (C) Particle size distribution (D) XRD of Co2+-CDs. XPS spectra of Co2+-CDs; (E) High resolution spectra of C 1s; (F) N 1s; (G) O 1s and (H) Co 2p. (I) Absorbance and fluorescent emission spectra of Co2+-CDs (λex = 380 nm, λem = 436 nm), Inset: the photographs of the Co2+-CDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right).

(A) The TEM image of R-CDs, Inset: Particle size distribution and HRTEM image of R-CDs. (B) FTIR spectrum of R-CDs. XPS spectra of R-CDs; (C) High resolution spectra of C 1s; (D) N 1s and (E) O 1s. (F) Absorbance and fluorescent emission spectra of R-CDs (λex = 425 nm, λem = 671 nm), Inset: the photos of R-CDs under natural light (left) and 365 nm UV lamp.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs under different concentrations of NO2 −, Inset: fluorescent image of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs with the addition of different concentrations of NO2 −. (B) Plot of F436/F671 versus NO2 − concentration, Inset: the calibration curve between the fluorescent intensity ratio F436/F671 and NO2 − concentration. (C) UV–vis spectra of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs upon incremental addition of NO2 −, Inset: visual image of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs with different NO2 − concentration. (D) Plot of absorption spectra of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs versus NO2 − concentration and the relationship of the UV–vis intensity and NO2 −.
Effect of relative metal ions (100 μM) or anions (100 μM) on fluorescent intensity ratio of F436/F671 and UV–vis absorption of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs in existence and absence of NO2 − (100 μM) in pH = 3.

(A) TEM of Co2+-CDs + NO2 −. (B) C 1s (C) N 1s and (D) Co 2p XPS spectra of Co2+-CDs + NO2 −. (E) Fluorescence lifetime of Co2+-CDs and Co2+-CDs + NO2 −. (F) Zeta potential of Co2+-CDs, NO2 − and Co2+-CDs + NO2 −. (G) CV curves of Co2+-CDs and Co2+-CDs + NO2 −. (H) The CIE color coordinates of (a) Co2+-CDs@R-CDs and (b) Co2+-CDs@R-CDs + NO2 − and (I) FTIR spectrum of Co2+-CDs and Co2+-CDs + NO2 −.

(A) Schematic diagram of paper strip coated with Co2+-CDs@R-CDs combined with a smartphone platform to detect NO2 − in dual modes. (B) Photos of paper strip coated with Co2+-CDs@R-CDs under UV lamp and natural light to detect NO2 −. (C) The fitting curve between R/B values and NO2 − (2.5–45 μM) in fluorescence mode. (D) The fitting curve between R/B values and NO2 − (2.5–60 μM) in colorimetric mode.

head.ce:title: Sensitive and reliable hybrid nanosensor (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) for ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detecting NO2 −

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Bialogical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Bialogical Engineering
University of Science and Technology Beijing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Bialogical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Chemistry, Henan Academy of Sciences, Zhengzhou 450002, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Chemistry, Henan Academy of Sciences, Zhengzhou 450002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric fluorescent and colorimetric detecting assay for NO2 − was realized by a hybrid nanosensor (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) utilizing firstly through the redox reaction of nitrite (NO2 −) with Co2+, of which the hybrid nanosensor Co2+-CDs@R-CDs was fabricated by Co2+-doped carbon dots (Co2+-CDs) and a reference of red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs). The ratiometric fluorescent linear detection range of NO2 − was 2.5–45 μM and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.068 μM with the response time of 120 s. While, the colorimetric linear detection range of NO2 − was 2.5–60 μM and the LOD was 0.075 μM. In addition, a portable smartphone system which could measure the R (red), G (green), and B (blue) values of the fluorescence and the visible color of the coated Co2+-CDs@R-CDs paper strip-based sensor had also been developed and successfully applied to detect NO2 − in sausage, pickles and tap water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional single-emission sensors have some application in detection now. However, these probes show poor signal specificity and sensitivity with signal-to background ratio, and the biggest challenge of these probes is their absolute intensity-dependent signal readout. In order to overcome the above shortcomings, some methods of ratiometric sensor with built-in self-calibration for signal intensity correction are applied and have led to more reliable and sensitive sensing. The ratiometric sensors often use the signals of one channel as a reference to correct and standardize the signals of other channels [1–3]. That is, to a certain extent, the ratiometric sensor has a self-calibration function, which can calibrate the interference caused by instrument fluctuations and other factors, and improve the accuracy and reliability of quantitative detection results [4]. Additionally, compared with the traditional single-mode sensor, the dual-mode sensor can also further improve the accuracy and reliability of the detection results by comparing the two modes, and has vast applied vistas in the detection of various ions [5,6], organic molecules [7] and biological imaging [8]. Therefore, ratiometric sensors with double modes have greater advantages in the field of sensing, and have attracted more and more research interests.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are novel type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterials [9,10], which have the characteristics of easy synthesis, good biocompatibility, good water dispersion, and widespread adoption in chemical analysis [11], biological imaging [12] and biosensing [13]. Among them, metal-doped carbon quantum dots can be used to adjust the band structure of carbon dots by adding metal dopants [14], such as Cu2+ and Fe3+ [15,16], to entitle them with new functionality, such as better catalytic performance and more efficient detection ability. Biomass quantum dots (BQDs) are environmentally friendly and low-cost, which have attracted much attention [17].

NO2 − exists widely in environment, food, industry and physiological systems, and is an important intermediate product in the nitrogen cycle [18]. Excess ingestion of NO2 − can interfere with the body's oxygen transport system and severe impairment of hemoglobin's ability to exchange oxygen in the blood, resulting in irreversible methemoglobinemia [19], which is particularly harmful to pregnant women and infants [20]. Moreover, NO2 − can also produce carcinogenic nitrosamines by the reaction with secondary amines and amides in the stomach [21,22]. Therefore, it is of great significance to detect NO2 − content to ensure human food safety. While, the reported detection methods for NO2 − included ion chromatography [23], electrochemical method [24], ultraviolet spectrophotometry [25], fluorescence spectrometry [26] and so on at present. Among them, the spectroscopic methods have the advantages of simple operation and high sensitivity, which have a broad application foreground. Spectroscopic methods are mainly based on Griess reaction [27,28], nitrosation reaction [29], nitrification reaction [30] and redox reaction [31–35] now. Usually, the application of redox reaction to detect NO2 − are mostly based on oxidation of Fe2+ by NO2 − [31–34]. However, the redox reaction of Co2+ and NO2 − has not been utilized to identify and detect NO2 −.

Herein, we proposed a novel redox reaction in ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of NO2 − based on Co2+-CDs and R-CDs composites (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs). Co2+-doped carbon dots (Co2+-CDs) with blue fluorescence underwent redox with NO2 −, accompanied by fluorescence quenching, and the red fluorescence of R-CDs remained unchanged. Meanwhile, NO2 − was coordinated with Co3+-CDs, leading to an obvious and immediate change from light red to purple. The results showed that Co2+-CDs@R-CDs had good selectivity, fast response and high sensitivity to NO2 −, which could satisfy the practical requirement of sensing. Moreover, ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetry ensured the reliability and accuracy of the detection results. Subsequently, based on the nano-sensors designed above, the corresponding ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric paper strips provided an effective way to detect NO2 − conveniently and quickly. Therefore, Co2+-CDs@R-CDs hybrid nanosensor with simple preparation, low cost and high sensitivity provided a novel strategy for the rapid detection of NO2 − in food and environment in situ.
Reagents and instruments were provided in Supporting Information.

CoCl2 (0.0205 g) and Diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA) (0.116 g) were sufficiently dissolved in water (20 mL), stirred well, added to a hydrothermal reactor, and then heat in an oven at 190 °C for 7 h. After the reaction was completed, the reaction solution was cooled naturally. Next, the solution was centrifuged at 20000 rpm for 8 min to remove the precipitation, and supernatant containing Co2+-CDs was obtained. The supernatant containing Co2+-CDs was dialyzed through 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h, and finally the Co2+-CDs was obtained by freeze-drying the dialyzed solution.

The fresh leaves of Chinese rose were washed, dried at 100 °C and crushed. The powder (1.0 g) was immersed to ethanol (20 mL), stirred continuously for 4 h, and then filtered with 0.22 μm polyethersulfone membrane. The clarified liquid was transferred to 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene kettle, heated at 180 °C for 7 h and cooled to room temperature. The dark green solution was purified with mixture of ethyl acetate and petroleum ether as eluent by column chromatography to afford R-CDs as yellow-green solid.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs and the optimization condition for detecting NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: The hybrid nanosensor Co2+-CDs@ R-CDs was formed from Co2+-CDs solution (0.1 mL, 15 mg/mL), R-CDs (0.1 mL, 5 mg/mL) and 9.7 mL buffer solution (pH = 3), and NO2 − solution (0.1 mL, 10 mM) was injected into the above solution evenly. Subsequently, the optimal response time was determined when the fluorescence intensity was almost constant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis and fluorescent measurements of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: The mixture Co2+-CDs@R-CDs containing Co2+-CDs solution (0.1 mL, 15 mg/mL), R-CDs (0.1 mL, 5 mg/mL) and 9.7 mL solution (pH = 3) was prepared. And NO2 − solution (0.1 mL) of different concentrations was added to above solution and mixed uniformly. UV–vis absorption and fluorescent emission were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and specificity of detecting NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: To better understand the influence of each interfering ion on blank sample of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs, and the influence of all interfering ions on detecting of NO2 −, a series of common anions, cations and micromolecule (K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Al3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, HS−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, ACO−, NO3 −, ClO− and H2O2) were tested in the experimental conditions with the reference of blank solution, and the concentration was the same as that of NO2 −.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Portable detection of NO2 − via smartphone APP in double modes
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the portable device for NO2 − detection was shown in Fig. 7A. In the process, a smartphone-based color identifier APP (Colorshot) was utilized to quantitatively monitor the fluorescence and the visual color changes of identifying processes under 365 nm UV light and sunlight respectively. As the different concentrations of NO2 − (0–80 μM) were spiked to the test tube, a series of fluorescence and visual color images were obtained under 365 nm UV light irradiation and sunlight, then converted to different RGB values by color recognition APP and further detected NO2 − quantitatively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of NO2 − in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy of the detection method was verified by standard addition method of two types of sausage, pickles purchased from supermarkets and tap water from two cities. Mix pickles or sausages with an equal amount of water, crush in a tissue masher, ultrasound, and dilute with water. The supernatant was filtered with filter membrane (0.22 μm) and diluted immediately for subsequent determination. According to the above experimental method, the content of NO2 − in three samples was detected by fluorescence and colorimetric dual modes, and each sample was measured for 3 times in parallel.

Co2+-CDs were characterized by FT-IR, TEM, XRD and XPS. The organic functional groups on the surface of Co2+-CDs were studied by FT-IR (Fig. 2 A). The wide absorption bands at 3391 cm−1 and 3260 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, while the CH stretching vibrations of aromatic ring and alkyl group were located at 2973 cm−1. A peak of CO/CN was observed at 1590 cm−1, indicating the presence of groups containing N and O in the graphite. The peak at 1385 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of C-N and the deformation vibration of OH. In Fig. 2B, the TEM image exhibited that the Co2+-CDs was nearly spherical with good dispersion, and the average particle size was 3.5–5.0 nm (Fig. 2C). Besides, the XRD pattern of Co2+-CDs showed a typical broad noisy peak at 24.5° as shown in Fig. 2D, which was attributed to (002) crystallographic planes of the Co2+-CDs [36], further confirming the formation of Co2+-CDs.

In order to further study the composition of Co2+-CDs, the elemental information and chemical state were characterized by XPS. In the high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2E), Co2+-CDs had three peaks at 284.43 eV, 285.53 eV and 287.83 eV, corresponding to CC/CC, CN/CO and COOH respectively. The two distinct peaks in the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2F) were 399.39 eV (N(C)3) and 401.15 eV (NH). Two distinct peaks of the O 1s spectrum (530.82 eV and 531.73 eV) were attributed to CO and CO respectively (Fig. 2G). Moreover, the Co 2p spectrum exhibited two predominant peaks, which corresponded to the Co 2p2/3 and Co 2p1/2 spin–orbit peaks of Co respectively (Fig. 2H) [37]. The above results confirmed that Co2+-CDs was synthesized successfully.

As depicted in the Fig. 2I, the aqueous solution of Co2+-CDs was light red in visible light. Besides, Co2+-CDs showed bright blue fluorescence when excited by a 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2I). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of Co2+-CDs (Fig. 2I, black line) showed two major absorption peaks at 313 nm and 508 nm, attributed to π → π* and n → π* transitions respectively [38]. Co2+-CDs exhibited very strong fluorescence emission (436 nm) at the optimal excitation wavelength of 380 nm (Fig. 2I, red line) with fluorescence quantum yield of 3.9 %, and Co2+-CDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependence in the excitation wavelength range of 310–430 nm (Fig. S1). And the fluorescent signal stability was further explored at high potency NaCl and 4 h of UV radiation. As depicted in Fig. S2A and Fig. S2B, the fluorescent intensity was stable throughout at pH = 3 and pH = 7, which laid the foundations for precision sensing measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization and optical properties of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The TEM images (Fig. 3 A) exhibited that the average particle sizes of R-CDs were 3.48 nm. The HRTEM images (Fig. 3A, inset) revealed that R-CDs had a lattice fringe spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponded to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [39]. Besides, as shown in Fig. 3B, the absorption peaks at 1032 cm−1, 1651 cm−1 and 1718 cm−1 were derived from the stretching vibration of COC, the bending vibration of CC and the stretching vibration of CO respectively. The absorption peaks at 2846/2918 cm−1 and 3415 cm−1 were derived from CH stretching vibration and OH stretching vibration respectively.

In order to further confirm the elemental composition, chemical bonds and surface functional groups of R-CDs, the XPS spectra test was performed. The full spectrum of XPS showed that there were three elements C, O and N in R-CDs. Five characteristic peaks (Fig. 3C) were found in the XPS energy spectrum of C1s, respectively at 283.65 eV, 284.50 eV, 285.51 eV, 286.58 eV and 287.89 eV, which were derived from CC/CC, CN, CO, CO/CN and COOH. The peaks of pyridinic N (398.68 eV), pyrrolic N (399.58 eV) and graphitic N (401.98 eV) were found in the XPS spectra of N 1s (Fig. 3D). Additionally, the high-resolution energy spectrum at the O 1s position could be fitted to two peaks at 531.67 eV and 532.95 eV (Fig. 3E), corresponding to the CO and CO groups respectively [40]. The precursor of R-CDs, Chinese rose leaves, had four absorption peaks at 338 nm, 434 nm, 470 nm and 662 nm (Fig. S3A). And R-CDs showed two absorption peaks at 405 nm and 665 nm, which were different from Chinese rose leaves, and red emission with the band maximum at 671 nm (Fig. S3B). The fluorescence quantum yield of R-CDs was 13.7 %. It was worth mentioning that R-CDs had almost the same fluorescence emission peak as the Chinese rose leaves, which indicated that chlorophyll may be confined to the R-CDs skeleton as the main fluorescent group [41]. Generally, R-CDs with red fluorescence was successfully prepared.

As shown in Fig. S4, it was found that NO2 − could quench the fluorescence of Co2+-CDs, while R-CDs had almost no change in fluorescence intensity in the presence of high concentration NO2 − (100 μM). Therefore, using R-CDs as reference, the shortcomings of single-signal fluorescence probes were eliminated by doping Co2+-CDs and R-CDs, and ratio fluorescence and colorimetric dual-mode probe (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) for NO2 − was constructed.

The UV–vis spectrum of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs exhibited peaks from Co2+-CDs (313 nm and 508 nm) and R-CDs (405 nm), while the fluorescence spectra revealed characteristic emission peaks at 436 nm for Co2+-CDs and 671 nm for R-CDs (Fig. S5A), indicating that the composite process had no impact on their respective optical properties.

In addition, the effects of NaCl concentration and UV irradiation on fluorescence intensity ratio (F436/F671) at 436 nm and 671 nm were further investigated. As shown in Fig. S5B and Fig. S5C, F436/F671 basically remained unchanged with the increase of NaCl concentration or after continuous exposure to 365 nm UV lamp for 2 h, which proved that Co2+-CDs@R-CDs had good optical stability and could be used as optical sensor.

To obtain better analytical performance, various detection conditions, including temperature, pH and reaction time, were optimized. First, F436/F671 of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs was examined under different temperatures. As seen from Fig. S5D, when the temperature was 45 °C, F436/F671 was the biggest. But 25 °C (room temperature) was selected as the optimal temperature, considering the convenience of actual operation.

To get insight into the optimized pH for detecting NO2 − by Co2+-CDs@R-CDs, the fluorescent intensity changes of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs in different pH values and before and after the addition of NO2 − were investigated. As could be seen from Fig. S6A, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F436/F671) of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs solution decreased with incremental pH values. As shown in Fig. S6B, the fluorescent intensity ratio (F436/F671) before and after detection were calculated, and ΔF436/F671 was the maximum when pH = 3. Therefore, pH = 3 was as the optimal pH value in the subsequent detection experiments. Response time was another crucial factor for better analytical performance. When the pH was 3, reaction time of 120 s could ensure that the ratio of fluorescent intensity became stable (Fig. S7), which was used as the optimal time for further NO2 − testing.

In general, the fluorescent intensity of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs at 436 nm decreased after the identification of NO2 −, while the fluorescent intensity at 671 nm remained unchanged, and the fluorescence color changed from blue to red. Meanwhile, the ultraviolet absorption peak at 508 nm reshifted to 538 nm with solution color from light red to purple (Fig. S8).

The time-dependent fluorescence response of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 − was evaluated by monitoring the change of fluorescence emission intensity (F436 and F671) of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs at 436 nm and 671 nm before and after the addition of NO2 −. As seen from Fig. S9, F436 and F671 of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs did not change significantly within 160 s without NO2 −. After adding NO2 − (100 μM), the fluorescence of F436 of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs changed rapidly with time, and then slowed down gradually, and the fluorescence intensity hardly changed when it reached 120 s. There was no significant change for F671 in its value within 160 s. It was further shown that the response time of probe Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to detect NO2 − was 120 s, which also indicated that it had good optical stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detection of NO2 -
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal reaction conditions, we studied the quantitative detection of NO2 − by Co2+-CDs@R-CDs based on fluorescence and colorimetric dual modes. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescent intensity at 436 nm and fluorescent intensity ratio (F436/F671) of the sensing system decreased with the increase of NO2 − concentration. In the range of 2.5–45 μM, the concentration of NO2 − displayed a good linear relationship with the ratio (Fig. 4B). Linear equation was F436/F671 = −0.04x + 2.30 (R2 = 0.9980), LOD was 0.068 μM, which was lower than that of some previously reported NO2 − detection method (Table S1).

In addition, it could be directly observed from Fig. 4C that the visual color of the solution of the sensing system changed from light red to purple upon adding NO2 −, and the ultraviolet absorption peak at 500–550 nm were continuously increased. There was a good linear relationship between the ultraviolet absorption peak and the concentration range of NO2 − (y = 0.00197x + 0.05323, R2 = 0.9996) between 2.5 and 60 μM (Fig. 4D). The calculated LOD was 0.075 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 − in solution
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity and anti-interference of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs to NO2 − were verified by possible interfering substances in complex environments, which included a series of common anions, cations and micromolecule (K+, Mg2+, Na+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Ba2+, Al3+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, CO3 2−, HCO3 −, SO4 2−, HS−, H2PO4 −, HPO4 2−, ACO−, NO3 −, ClO− and H2O2). As shown in Fig. 5 , in absence of NO2 −, there was no significant difference between the F436/F671 and UV absorption of the sample in the presence of interfering substances (except H2O2 and ClO−) and the blank sample, and when the interfering substances (except H2O2 and ClO−) coexisted with NO2 −, the obtained F436/F671 and UV absorption were similar to those in the presence of NO2 − only.

With the addition of ClO− and H2O2 alone, F436/F671 decreased slightly, but the UV absorption intensity of the system hardly changed. This may be because ClO− and H2O2 could have redox reaction with Co2+, affecting the fluorescence of Co2+-CDs.

When ClO− and H2O2 coexisted with NO2 −, the F436/F671 value was similar with that of NO2 − alone, and the UV absorption intensity of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs was slightly increased. This may be because ClO− or H2O2 could react with NO2 − as follows, consuming part of the excess NO2 −, and more Co3+ generated by the reaction of ClO− or H2O2 with Co2+ could coordinate with the remaining NO2 − in the system to produce purple products.
ClO− + NO2 − = NO3 − + Cl−
2NO2 − + 2H2O2 = 2NO3 − + 2OH− + O2

Overall, the presence of ClO− and H2O2 did not have a significant effect on the detection of NO2 − by combining fluorescence and colorimetric dual modes. So, it turned out that Co2+-CDs@R-CDs could identify NO2 − specifically in a complex environment with good selectivity and sensitivity.

The mechanism of response of Co2+-CDs to NO2 − may be based on the redox reaction of Co2+ and NO2 − under acidic conditions to produce Co3+, NO and H2O as shown below [42]. In order to further verify the mechanism of detecting NO2 − in fluorescence and colorimetry, TEM, XPS, gas chromatograph, fluorescent lifetime, Zeta, CV curves, CIE chromaticity diagram and FT-IR were tested.
Co2+ + 7NO2 − + 2H+ = Co(NO2)6 3+ + NO + H2O

Firstly, it could be seen from Fig. 6 A, Co2+-CDs remained spherical with good dispersion after detecting NO2 −. And the high-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s (Fig. 6B) and N 1s (Fig. 6C) had no change before and after identifying NO2 −. But the high-resolution XPS spectra of Co 2p (Fig. 6D) indicated the ratio of Co3+/Co2+ changed from 70.30 % to 83.97 % after identifying NO2 −. Thus, Co3+ ion could facilitate the non-radiative recombination of excited electron in conduction band and empty holes in valence band, further turning off the fluorescence of Co2+-CDs effectively [43]. Besides, it was verified by gas chromatograph that the gas produced in the detection process was NO (Table S2).

Further, the fluorescent lifetime of Co2+-CDs was 2.39 ns, and it changed to 2.28 ns upon adding NO2 − (Fig. 6E), suggesting that the fluorescence quenching process of NO2 − on Co2+-CDs should be static quenching. The zeta potentials of Co2+-CDs and NO2 − were 4.36 mV and −2.0 mV respectively, reflecting that there was electrostatic attraction between them. The zeta potential of the Co2+-CDs increased from 4.36 to 5.59 mV upon addition of NO2 − (Fig. 6F), suggesting that the stability of Co2+-CDs was further improved following NO2 − addition [44]. Besides, to better understand the interactions of Co2+-CDs with NO2 −, the process was investigated by cyclic voltammetry (Fig. 6G). On a CHI660E electrochemical workstation, cyclic voltammetry experiments were conducted using a traditional three-electrode system, consisting of platinum wire as the counter electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum sheet as the working electrode. After adding NO2 −, a1 and a2 peaks appeared and the current intensity increased, reflecting the existence of redox reaction [45]. Therefore, it was concluded that the oxidation reaction between NO2 − and Co2+, which could convert Co2+ to Co3+ and turn off the fluorescence of Co2+-CDs.

Based on it, the fluorescence color changes were marked in the CIE diagram. After identifying NO2 −, the CIE color coordinates of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs changed from a (0.17, 0.15) to b (0.31, 0.19) (Fig. 6H), which further was in accordance with the spectral variation coordinates [46].

On the other hand, the possible mechanism of detecting NO2 − in colorimetric mode was studied. After the identification of NO2 −, the infrared characteristic absorption peaks OH (3400 cm−1), CO (1640 cm−1) and CN (1382 cm−1) of Co2+-CDs redshifted to 3431 cm−1, 1644 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 respectively (Fig. 6I). Meanwhile, the absorption peak in the ultraviolet absorption spectrum of Co2+-CDs was redshifted from 508 nm to 538 nm, which should be due to the coordination between NO2 − and Co3+ (Fig. S8B).

Furthermore, the mixed sensing system (Co2+-CDs@R-CDs) was combined with a color analysis APP of smart phone to achieve more accurate visual detection in dual modes. Fig. 7 B showed that the fluorescence of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs coated on the paper strip changed from blue to red with the concentration of NO2 − under ultraviolet lamp. According to the information of “R/B” value and NO2 − concentration, a standard curve as shown in Fig. 7C was established. It can be seen that “R/B” value displayed a good linear correlation with NO2 − concentration within the range of 2.5–45 μM. The linear equation was y = 0.00456x + 0.529 (R2 = 0.9944). Meanwhile, the color of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs system coated on the paper strip changed from light red to purple with the increased concentration of NO2 − (Fig. 7B). While, the linear equations between the R/B value in colorimetric mode and NO2 − were y = −0.0018x + 1.108 (R2 = 0.9986) with the range of 2.5–60 μM (Fig. 7D).

To further evaluate the practicability of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs for detecting NO2 −, three kinds of samples (sausage, pickles and tap water) were selected for experiments by standard addition method. In order to ensure the accuracy of experimental results, two samples from different brands or cities were selected for each kind of sample. Fluorescent and colorimetric analysis of actual samples were performed by smartphone-based assay (Table S3–S5). The recoveries of the ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric results of the actual samples were 98.4 %–101.6 % and 99.0 %–102.4 % respectively, which exhibited reliability and great potential for the on-site analysis of real NO2 − samples.

In summary, a hybrid nanosensor of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs had been constructed by using R-CDs as an internal reference fluorescent reagent, by which a ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric detecting assay had realized for NO2 −. The as-synthesized Co2+-CDs interacted with NO2 − to product Co3+, which caused the fluorescence color of Co2+-CDs changing from blue to none by static quenching, while R-CDs red-emitting was insensitive. Ultimately, a clear fluorescence result of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs showed from blue to red with a sensitive LOD of 0.068 μM, and had a linear relationship in the range of 2.5–45 μM with NO2 −. In the process, the visual colors of the system also changed from light red to purple, and displayed a linear relationship from 2.5 to 60 μM with NO2 −concentrations, and the LOD was 0.075 μM. Besides, the hybrid nanosensor of Co2+-CDs@R-CDs as well as the Co2+-CDs@R-CDs-based paper strip sensor with smartphone had been successfully developed and had been used for monitoring NO2 − in the real samples such as sausage, pickles and tap water, and had showed that they could be used for quantitative, sensitive and selective detection of NO2 − with great potential applications in food and environment filed.

Xiaohui Hao: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Ao Shen: Project administration, Methodology. Ruochen Duan: Conceptualization. Panqing Zhang: Resources. Xiuqing Zhao: Validation. Xuebing Wang: Visualization. Xue Li: Investigation. Zeyu Zhang: Software. Yunxu Yang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the National Science Foundation of China (No. 21575012), the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (No. 2212010), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (FRF-BR-19-003B).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124661.

ce:section.ce:para:
